---
title: "Swift Concurrency and Ownership"
---

The application has three timing domains: the real-time audio callback, the
serialized Voxtral inference queue, and the main actor. Gemma and networking
add asynchronous work, but they must never block the first two.

## Suspension is not a thread

`async` means a function may suspend. `await` marks a point where the current
task can yield while another operation progresses. It does not promise a new
thread.

```swift
let drainedText = await embeddedVoxtral.stop()
```

The caller suspends until `stop` resumes its continuation. UI event processing
can continue because no thread is synchronously waiting.

## Tasks carry cancellation and actor context

```swift
correctionWorkerTask = Task { [weak self] in
    guard let self else { return }
    while !Task.isCancelled, !self.pendingCorrections.isEmpty {
        let pending = self.pendingCorrections.removeFirst()
        await self.correctSegment(pending.segmentID, mode: pending.mode)
    }
    self.correctionWorkerTask = nil
}
```

This worker serializes corrections by awaiting one request before removing the
next. Serialization is application policy: it bounds memory, preserves lecture
order, and prevents simultaneous 26B-model requests from fighting for GPU
resources.

Cancellation is cooperative. `cancel()` sets a flag; code must check
`Task.isCancelled` or call an operation that throws `CancellationError`.

## The main actor owns application and UI state

```swift
@MainActor
@Observable
final class ClassroomAppModel { ... }
```

Every isolated property access and method call belongs to the main actor.
Compiler diagnostics catch many accidental background mutations. AppKit and
SwiftUI also expect their objects to be manipulated from the main thread, so
this language-level boundary matches the platform boundary.

Callbacks arriving from non-main queues explicitly hop:

```swift
Task { @MainActor [weak self] in
    self?.handleVoxtralEvent(event)
}
```

## A serial dispatch queue owns the C runtime

The embedded service uses:

```swift
private let inferenceQueue = DispatchQueue(
    label: "ClassroomCaptions.voxtral.inference",
    qos: .userInitiated
)
```

Model load, stream creation, audio feed, token drain, flush, and destruction
all execute on this queue. Therefore `vox_stream_t` never receives concurrent
calls. This is stronger and easier to audit than placing separate locks around
individual C calls.

The small `State` structure is separately protected:

```swift
private let state = Mutex(State())

let stream = state.withLock { $0.stream }
```

The lock protects publication and lookup of handles and the event
continuation. It does not make the C object internally thread-safe. The serial
queue still owns operations on the pointee.

## Checked continuations adapt callback APIs

```swift
try await withCheckedThrowingContinuation {
    (continuation: CheckedContinuation<Void, Error>) in
    inferenceQueue.async {
        // perform synchronous C setup
        continuation.resume()
    }
}
```

A continuation is a one-shot capability to resume the suspended async
function. “Checked” means the runtime diagnoses common mistakes such as
abandoning or resuming it twice. Every branch must resume exactly once.

## `AsyncStream` adapts a sequence of callbacks

Transcription produces many events, so one continuation is retained:

```swift
func events() -> AsyncStream<TranscriptionEvent> {
    AsyncStream { continuation in
        state.withLock { $0.continuation = continuation }
        continuation.onTermination = { [weak self] _ in
            self?.state.withLock { $0.continuation = nil }
        }
    }
}
```

The producer calls `yield(event)`. The app model consumes the stream with
`for await`. Termination clears the retained continuation and prevents a stale
subscriber from being called.

## Real-time audio has stricter rules

The Core Audio callback cannot await, allocate unpredictably, log heavily, or
touch main-actor state. It renders into preallocated memory and hands bounded
work to the rest of the pipeline. The callback is a deadline-driven C ABI
entry point even though its implementation is in Swift.

Conceptually:

```text
Core Audio thread -> render PCM -> bounded callback -> inference queue
                                            \-> archive queue
```

Pause closes the capture gate before buffered audio is discarded. This keeps
paused speech out of both Voxtral and the optional WAV archive.

## Concurrency proof for a caption

1. Core Audio invokes the render callback.
2. PCM data is copied into owned `Data`; no callback pointer escapes.
3. `sendAudio` enqueues the owned bytes on `inferenceQueue`.
4. The queue calls the C bridge and drains complete text tokens.
5. `AsyncStream` publishes a value-type event.
6. The observer hops to `MainActor`.
7. The app model mutates its value-type session and refreshes the overlay.
8. A finalized segment enters the one-at-a-time correction task.

No UI object enters the audio callback. No C stream call occurs on the main
actor. No Gemma request delays provisional text.

## Failure patterns to avoid

- Calling C stream functions from both `stop()` and `sendAudio()` without one
  owner queue creates a use-after-free race.
- Holding a mutex while awaiting can deadlock or retain mutable state across an
  arbitrary suspension.
- Capturing `self` strongly in a callback retained by `self` creates a cycle.
- Assuming task cancellation interrupts C inference is incorrect; the C layer
  needs its own cancellation check if interruption is required.
- Marking a type `@unchecked Sendable` without documenting its synchronization
  transfers correctness from the compiler to the programmer.
